meta.title: fak intent-literal scorecard - does a metric's name invite an intent its definition delivers
meta._doc: Each row is one reported metric, positioned on whether its NAME invites a wider intent than its LITERAL definition delivers - worst of all a SELF-REFERENTIAL denominator ('how much we do of X' where X is our own positioned set, not the external market) - and, if it diverges, whether the surface DISCLOSES the gap where a reader sees it. The scorecard CROSS-CHECKS the tree: the grounding token must appear in the named surface, and a divergent row's disclosure phrase must literally appear in that surface, so coverage cannot be gamed by editing JSON. Distinct from concept-disambiguation (do two NAMES blur) and conflation (a number's PROVENANCE). Regenerate the doc: python tools/intent_literal_scorecard.py --markdown-dir docs/intent-literal-scorecard

surfaces.name: Gateway Prometheus metrics
surfaces.note: The operator-facing /metrics surface. HELP text is where a divergent denominator is disclosed (or not).

surfaces.name: Industry scorecard headline
surfaces.note: The OUTWARD competitive map. Its 'coverage' and 'composite score' are self-referential (our taxonomy), fenced by 'Read this right'.

surfaces.name: Concept-disambiguation scorecard headline
surfaces.note: The INWARD naming map. Its 'coverage' denominator is the whole discovered confusable space - the honest fix to the self-referential framing industry's number exposes.

surfaces.name: Issue-dispatch status headline
surfaces.note: The fleet operations surface. closure_rate is an HONESTY ratio, not throughput; the formula is printed inline.
